_comment: Unified Social Media content templates — LinkedIn, Facebook, Twitter, Instagram — AI Employee Vault Gold Tier

facebook.media: optional — photos get 2.3x more engagement than text posts
key_facts: Short posts (1-80 chars) have highest engagement for text-only
Photos with text get 2.3x more engagement than plain text
Posts between 5pm-1am get lowest reach — avoid late night
Native video outperforms YouTube links by 3x in organic reach
Emoji in the first line boosts reach by up to 57%

instagram.media: REQUIRED — image or video is mandatory

templates:
Unpopular opinion: most AI tools fail not because of the AI — but because of the workflow around it.

Here's what I mean:

You can't bolt AI onto a broken process and expect magic. The AI just automates the chaos.

What actually works:
1/ Map the workflow FIRST
2/ Identify the decision points that need human judgment
3/ Only then add AI for the rest

The teams winning with AI aren't moving faster — they're moving cleaner.

What's one workflow you'd redesign if you started from scratch today?

After building an AI Employee system, here's the most surprising thing I've learned:

The hardest part isn't the AI.
It's deciding what the AI should NOT do.

{{details}}

What decisions in your workflow should always stay human?

Most AI implementations fail not because of the AI.

They fail because the workflow was broken before AI touched it.

You can't automate chaos. First fix the process. 🧵

Hot take: the most important AI skill isn't prompting.

It's knowing what NOT to automate.

Some decisions need a human. Full stop.

The AI lesson nobody talks about:

The tool is never the problem.
The workflow around the tool is.

Before you add AI, fix the process. Then automate it.

What's one thing AI has actually helped you do better? Tell me in the comments 👇

Counterintuitive truth about automation:

The goal isn't to eliminate work. It's to eliminate friction so better work can happen.

Biggest AI win this month for us: {{win}}

What's yours? 💬

templates:
3 things that made our AI automation actually work (most teams skip #2):

1. **Human-in-the-loop first.** Every automated action that touches the outside world requires a human sign-off before it executes. No exceptions.

2. **Audit trail everything.** If you can't explain what the AI did and why, you've lost control. Log every action.

3. **Start small, prove value, then expand.** We started with email triage. Only after that worked did we add scheduling, reporting, and social posting.

Which one resonates most with where your team is right now?

3 rules for AI automation that actually works:

1. Human approval before external actions
2. Log everything (if you can't audit it, you can't trust it)
3. Start small, prove value, expand

Most teams skip rule 2. Don't. 🧵

Quick tip for building with AI:

Don't start with "what can AI do?"

Start with "what takes 20% of my time and requires 0% of my judgment?"

That's your first automation. Go.

AI automation tip that actually works 👇

Before you automate anything, ask:
"Does this decision need human judgment?"

If YES → keep human in the loop
If NO → automate it

That simple filter saves you from every AI horror story you've heard.

Save this for your next automation project 📌

3-second productivity check:

Look at your task list.
Highlight everything that requires your actual brain.

Everything else?
That's your automation backlog.

What's on your list right now? 👇
instagram.caption_note: Pair with a clean checklist graphic or split-image comparing before/after

templates:
Here's how our AI Employee actually works — no hype, just the real architecture:

**The Vault** — an Obsidian folder that acts as the AI's inbox, memory, and action log.

**The Loop** — Ralph (our autonomous agent) runs on an OODA cycle: Observe → Orient → Decide → Act → Reflect.

**The Check** — nothing touches the outside world without a human review. Every approval is logged.

**The Output** — emails handled, reports generated, LinkedIn posts drafted, CEO briefings written.

The whole thing runs on files. No database. No cloud dependency.

What's the simplest AI system you've seen actually work in production?

Behind the scenes of our AI Employee system:

• Inbox = Obsidian folder
• Brain = OODA loop agent
• Safety = human-in-the-loop for every external action
• Memory = plain markdown files

Simplest architecture that actually works. 🧵

Our AI Employee runs on markdown files.

No database. No cloud dependency. No magic.

Just a well-designed folder structure and clear rules for the AI.

Sometimes simple beats sophisticated.

Behind the curtain: what our AI Employee actually runs on.

Not a fancy cloud platform.
Not an expensive enterprise tool.

A folder. A loop. And clear rules about when humans stay in control.

The best systems are the ones you can explain in 30 seconds.

What's the simplest tool in your stack that does the most work? 👇

Real talk about building with AI:

We spent 20% of our time on the AI.
We spent 80% on the workflow around it.

That ratio is the secret.

The AI is only as good as the system it lives in.

Builders: what's your workflow-to-AI ratio? 🤔
instagram.caption_note: Pair with a architecture diagram screenshot or clean workspace photo

linkedin: Hook in first 2 lines — must earn the 'see more' click
Use white space and line breaks for readability
End with a question or CTA to drive engagement
Post Tue-Thu 8-10am or 12-1pm for maximum reach
Native video gets 3x more reach than YouTube links
Respond to every comment within the first hour
twitter: Hook in first 5 words — Twitter feeds move fast
Threads outperform single tweets for complex topics
Use 1-2 hashtags max — too many looks spammy
End threads with a clear CTA or question
Alt text on images improves accessibility and reach
Quote-tweet your own thread to boost engagement
facebook: Keep text posts under 80 chars for highest engagement
Photos with captions get 2.3x more reach than text-only
End with a direct question to drive comments
Post Wed-Sun between 1-4pm for organic reach
Native video outperforms YouTube links by 3x
Avoid posting links in the main post — put them in the first comment
Emoji in the first line can boost reach by up to 57%
instagram: Image/video quality is non-negotiable — blurry = ignored
First 125 chars of caption show without 'more' — make them count
20-25 hashtags in first comment (not caption) avoids spammy look
Stories get 15x more interaction than feed posts
Reels get 22% more reach than standard video posts
Use location tags to boost local discoverability
